GarbageCollectingList.brief: A container class that manages a list of object pointers with integrated garbage collection and automatic cleanup.
GarbageCollectingList.details: The GarbageCollectingList class is a specialized container designed to manage pointers to objects, providing built-in garbage collection capabilities. It internally uses a std::list to store elements and offers methods to add objects, mark them as garbage, and remove garbage objects efficiently. The class features a custom iterator that automatically skips over objects marked as garbage during traversal, simplifying iteration over valid elements. It supports operations such as adding new objects, marking objects as garbage for deferred cleanup, and periodically deleting garbage objects to free memory. The class ensures type safety through compile-time checks and provides copy constructors and assignment operators for flexible management of object collections. Its design is suitable for systems requiring explicit lifecycle management, resource recycling, or cache management, where objects may become invalid over time and need to be cleaned up systematically.
GarbageCollectingList.notes: The class employs a custom iterator that skips garbage objects during iteration, facilitating efficient traversal and cleanup. It follows the RAII pattern for resource management, ensuring proper cleanup of dynamically allocated objects. The design enforces type safety through compile-time checks, preventing misuse with incompatible types.
GarbageCollectingList.rationale: This class exists to provide an efficient and safe mechanism for managing object lifecycles in systems where objects can become obsolete or invalid over time. By integrating garbage marking and cleanup within a single container, it simplifies resource management, reduces memory leaks, and improves performance in scenarios such as caching, resource pooling, or simulation environments where objects frequently change state or are removed.
GarbageCollectingList.name: GarbageCollectingList

usages.class_garbage_collecting_list_1_1iterato: Used as a reference to the list that the iterator operates on, enabling access to the list's management functions or state within the iterator's methods.

container.brief: The list container that holds all elements managed by the GarbageCollectingList class.
container.details: This variable is a standard linked list that stores all elements within the GarbageCollectingList, including both garbage and non-garbage items. It facilitates efficient insertion at the front, iteration, and removal of elements during garbage collection processes. The container is initialized empty and dynamically updates as elements are added or removed through class methods. Its iterators are used extensively for traversing the list, checking the number of stored elements, and managing element lifecycle operations.
container.notes: The container maintains the order of insertion, with new elements typically added to the front. Proper iterator management is essential during modifications to avoid invalidation. The list's structure supports efficient element removal, which is crucial during garbage collection.
container.rationale: This container exists to manage the collection of elements, enabling efficient garbage detection, removal, and addition operations, thereby supporting the core functionality of the GarbageCollectingList class.
container.name: container

Iterated over in a loop to examine each element; used to access and potentially delete elements based on their 'is_garbage()' status, with elements being removed from the container if they are identified as garbage.

iterator GarbageCollectingList< T >::beg.brief: Returns an iterator to the first non-garbage element in the list, skipping any initial garbage elements.
iterator GarbageCollectingList< T >::beg.details: This function creates an iterator starting at the beginning of the internal container. It then checks if this iterator points to a garbage element. If so, it advances the iterator to skip over consecutive garbage elements, ensuring that the returned iterator points to the first valid (non-garbage) element. If the container is empty or all elements are garbage, it returns an iterator to the end of the list. This approach simplifies iteration by automatically bypassing garbage elements, allowing clients to iterate over only the valid data without manual checks.
iterator GarbageCollectingList< T >::beg.returns: An iterator to the first non-garbage element, or the end iterator if none exists.
iterator GarbageCollectingList< T >::beg.notes: The returned iterator is designed to work seamlessly with standard iteration patterns, providing efficient traversal over non-garbage elements. It assumes that the container and iterator are properly initialized and that the garbage status of elements is maintained correctly.
iterator GarbageCollectingList< T >::beg.rationale: This function is used when iterating over the list to process only valid elements, especially in contexts where garbage collection is performed periodically. It ensures that iteration skips over elements marked as garbage, improving efficiency and correctness in list processing routines.
iterator GarbageCollectingList< T >::beg.name: begin

iterator GarbageCollectingList< T >::end.brief: Returns an iterator pointing to the position just past the last element of the list.
iterator GarbageCollectingList< T >::end.details: This function creates and returns an iterator initialized with the container's end iterator and a reference to the current GarbageCollectingList instance. The returned iterator facilitates traversal over the list while skipping elements marked as garbage, as defined by the iterator's internal logic. It serves as the standard end marker in iteration patterns, enabling clients to detect when iteration has reached the end or to compare the current position during traversal.
iterator GarbageCollectingList< T >::end.returns: An iterator representing the position just past the last element, used as the end marker in iteration.
iterator GarbageCollectingList< T >::end.notes: The returned iterator is designed to work seamlessly with the list's garbage collection logic, ensuring that iteration can efficiently skip over invalid or garbage elements.
iterator GarbageCollectingList< T >::end.rationale: This function is used to obtain the end iterator for iteration loops, allowing clients to traverse the list safely and efficiently, especially in contexts where garbage elements may be present and need to be skipped.
iterator GarbageCollectingList< T >::end.name: end

usages.class_garbage_collecting_list_1_1iterato: Called to obtain the end iterator of the list, serving as the boundary condition for the traversal loop.

GarbageCollectingList< T >::GarbageColle.brief: Default constructor that performs a compile-time check for type compatibility.
GarbageCollectingList< T >::GarbageColle.details: This constructor initializes a GarbageCollectingList object and enforces type safety by performing a static cast of a null pointer of type T to a Garbage pointer. This check occurs at compile time, ensuring that the template type T is convertible to a Garbage pointer, which is essential for the list's correct functioning within garbage management systems. The cast does not produce any runtime overhead but serves as a safeguard against incompatible types, preventing potential errors during resource management operations.

tparams.T: The type of objects stored in the list, which must be compatible with Garbage.

GarbageCollectingList< T >::GarbageColle.notes: The constructor does not initialize any data members explicitly; its primary purpose is to enforce type constraints at compile time. This pattern helps maintain type safety in generic garbage collection structures.
GarbageCollectingList< T >::GarbageColle.rationale: This constructor is used when creating a list of objects that are expected to be managed within a garbage collection system, ensuring that only appropriate types are used and preventing runtime errors related to type incompatibility.
GarbageCollectingList< T >::GarbageColle.name: GarbageCollectingList

virtual GarbageCollectingList< T >::~Gar.brief: Virtual destructor for the GarbageCollectingList class to ensure proper cleanup of resources.
virtual GarbageCollectingList< T >::~Gar.details: This destructor is declared as virtual to facilitate correct polymorphic cleanup when deleting objects through base class pointers. Although it has an empty implementation, its virtual designation guarantees that destructors of derived classes are invoked appropriately, preventing resource leaks and ensuring proper resource management. This design is essential in inheritance hierarchies where derived classes may allocate resources that need explicit release during destruction.
virtual GarbageCollectingList< T >::~Gar.returns: This function does not return a value.
virtual GarbageCollectingList< T >::~Gar.notes: Having a virtual destructor is crucial in polymorphic base classes to avoid undefined behavior during object destruction. Even if the destructor body is empty, its virtual nature ensures proper cleanup in derived classes.
virtual GarbageCollectingList< T >::~Gar.rationale: This destructor is used when objects of derived classes are deleted through base class pointers, ensuring that all resources are correctly released and preventing potential memory leaks.
virtual GarbageCollectingList< T >::~Gar.name: ~GarbageCollectingList

size_t GarbageCollectingList< T >::size(.brief: Returns the number of elements currently stored in the list.
size_t GarbageCollectingList< T >::size(.details: This method accesses the internal container that manages all list elements and retrieves its size, reflecting the total count of items present. As a const function, it ensures no modification to the object's state during execution. The implementation directly delegates to the container's size() method, ensuring efficient retrieval of the element count.
size_t GarbageCollectingList< T >::size(.returns: The total number of elements in the list as a size_t.
size_t GarbageCollectingList< T >::size(.notes: Since the function is const and delegates directly to the container's size() method, it provides a quick and thread-safe way to query the list size without modifying the list.
size_t GarbageCollectingList< T >::size(.rationale: This function is useful whenever the caller needs to determine how many elements are currently managed by the list, such as for iteration bounds, capacity checks, or reporting.
size_t GarbageCollectingList< T >::size(.name: size

void GarbageCollectingList< T >::add(T p.brief: Adds a new element to the front of the container and marks it as active.
void GarbageCollectingList< T >::add(T p.details: This function inserts the provided pointer 'ptr' at the beginning of the internal container, ensuring that the most recently added element is prioritized for processing or access. After insertion, it calls the 'not_garbage' method on the object pointed to by 'ptr', indicating that the object is now active and should not be considered for garbage collection. The method assumes that 'ptr' is valid and that the object supports the 'not_garbage' method, which manages the object's garbage collection state to prevent premature deletion.

params.ptr: A pointer to the object to be added; must have a 'not_garbage' method.

void GarbageCollectingList< T >::add(T p.returns: This function does not return a value.
void GarbageCollectingList< T >::add(T p.notes: The function relies on the object pointed to by 'ptr' implementing 'not_garbage', which is essential for correct garbage collection management. It is typically used when new objects are created or reactivated to ensure they are tracked as active elements.
void GarbageCollectingList< T >::add(T p.rationale: This method is used when adding new or reactivated objects to the collection, ensuring they are marked as active and prioritized within the container, facilitating efficient garbage collection and object management.
void GarbageCollectingList< T >::add(T p.name: add

void GarbageCollectingList< T >::remove(.brief: Marks the specified object as garbage for deferred cleanup.
void GarbageCollectingList< T >::remove(.details: The remove function calls the make_garbage() method on the object pointed to by ptr, indicating that it should be scheduled for later deletion. This design relies on objects implementing make_garbage() to update their internal state accordingly. By deferring actual deletion, the system can perform batch cleanup operations, which can improve performance and resource management, especially when dealing with multiple objects requiring cleanup at once.

params.ptr: A pointer to an object that supports the make_garbage() method, marking it for deferred deletion.

void GarbageCollectingList< T >::remove(.throws: This function does not throw exceptions; it assumes ptr is a valid, dereferenceable pointer.
void GarbageCollectingList< T >::remove(.notes: This method relies on the object's make_garbage() implementation to correctly update its state. It is typically used in scenarios where immediate deletion is undesirable, such as during iteration or batch processing.
void GarbageCollectingList< T >::remove(.rationale: Used in contexts where objects need to be marked for cleanup without immediate deletion, allowing for safe deferred resource management, as seen in character removal or socket cleanup scenarios.
void GarbageCollectingList< T >::remove(.name: remove

usages.comm_8cc, void close_socket(Descriptor *: Called to mark objects as garbage if the character is an NPC without a descriptor, for cleanup.
usages.class_world, void World::remove_char(Cha: Called to mark the character object pointed to by ch as garbage, effectively scheduling it for deletion.

void GarbageCollectingList< T >::delete_.brief: Removes and deletes all objects marked as garbage from the internal container.
void GarbageCollectingList< T >::delete_.details: The delete_garbage() function iterates through the container holding pointers to managed objects, checking each with the is_garbage() method to identify objects that are marked as garbage. When such objects are found, they are explicitly deleted to free associated memory, and their pointers are removed from the container to maintain consistency. The iteration carefully manages iterator validity during removal to ensure all garbage objects are properly cleaned up. This process helps prevent memory leaks and keeps the container free of obsolete objects, facilitating efficient memory management in scenarios where objects are periodically marked as garbage and require cleanup.
void GarbageCollectingList< T >::delete_.returns: This function does not return a value.
void GarbageCollectingList< T >::delete_.throws: No exceptions are explicitly thrown by this function.
void GarbageCollectingList< T >::delete_.notes: The function assumes that all pointers in the container are valid and that deleting an object is safe. It relies on the is_garbage() method to accurately identify objects eligible for deletion.
void GarbageCollectingList< T >::delete_.rationale: This function is used in scenarios where objects are marked as garbage over time, such as during game updates or simulation steps, to efficiently clean up unused or obsolete objects and manage memory effectively.
void GarbageCollectingList< T >::delete_.name: delete_garbage

usages.class_world, void World::update(): Called at the end of the function to remove and delete garbage objects from char_list.

GarbageCollectingList< T >::GarbageColle.brief: Copy constructor for the GarbageCollectingList class.
GarbageCollectingList< T >::GarbageColle.details: This constructor creates a new instance of the GarbageCollectingList by copying the contents and state of an existing GarbageCollectingList object. It ensures that the new list is a deep copy, meaning that it duplicates the elements and any associated resources, rather than just copying pointers or references.
GarbageCollectingList< T >::GarbageColle.returns: This constructor does not return a value as it is used to initialize a new instance of the class.
GarbageCollectingList< T >::GarbageColle.name: GarbageCollectingList

GarbageCollectingList & GarbageCollectin.brief: Assigns the contents of one GarbageCollectingList to another.
GarbageCollectingList & GarbageCollectin.details: This copy assignment operator allows one GarbageCollectingList to be assigned the contents of another. It ensures that the current list is properly cleared and all resources are managed correctly before copying the elements from the source list. This operation may involve deallocating existing elements and copying or moving elements from the source list, depending on the implementation.

params.rhs: The source GarbageCollectingList whose contents are to be copied.

GarbageCollectingList & GarbageCollectin.returns: A reference to the current GarbageCollectingList after assignment.
GarbageCollectingList & GarbageCollectin.name: operator=
